topic: A person's longtime hobby becomes their child's obsession
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of linseed oil and aged canvas used to be a comforting blanket, a quiet sanctuary where I could let my mind drift. Now, it’s a constant, low hum of anxiety. Leo, my seven-year-old, has taken my meticulous watercolor practice and twisted it into a full-blown crusade. Every time I set up my palette, I feel a tightening in my chest, a need to check the window, to listen for footsteps that aren't his. He insists that the light hitting the dust motes in the studio at precisely 4:17 PM is the 'perfect illumination' for capturing the ephemeral nature of existence. I watch him now, his small hands hovering over the paper, his brow furrowed in intense concentration, and a cold knot forms beneath my ribs. I keep glancing toward the door, a habit born of a deep-seated unease.

[story 2]

"Mom, look! The shadow on the wall is moving!" Leo’s voice, bright and utterly convinced, cut through the afternoon silence. He was standing beside my easel, his eyes wide and fixed on the corner of the room. I forced a smile, a practiced, thin thing that didn't reach my eyes. My fingers, usually steady when mixing pigments, felt clumsy, my knuckles white as I gripped the brush handle. It wasn't the light playing tricks; it was the way he interpreted everything, magnifying the mundane into something sinister, something that demanded his immediate, focused attention. I moved to adjust the blinds, my movements sharp and economical, cataloging every sound—the distant traffic, the creak of the floorboards—as if searching for an intrusion.

[story 3]

I spent an hour today just observing him. He wasn't painting anymore; he was mapping. He used charcoal to sketch intricate patterns on the floorboards, claiming they were the secret pathways of the house. When he showed me the latest diagram—a complex network of lines intersecting the baseboards—I felt a sudden, sharp instinct to retreat. My breath hitched. It was the sheer, unbridled intensity of his focus that unnerved me. It wasn't playful curiosity; it was an absorption, a deep, almost predatory need to decode the world around him through the lens of his chosen medium. I retreated to the kitchen, the clatter of dishes sounding deafeningly loud, trying to anchor myself against the swirling sense of being constantly appraised, constantly observed.
